message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 9 (29 todo)
Recent changes: Split items into runes/non-runes; runes priced with a special-case (exactly 3 → 60, else count×25); non-runes sum base premiums; + fee.

Refactor while keeping tests green. The rune-specific special-casing is a candidate for cleanup — evaluate naming, duplication, and whether the structure should generalize. But do not add behavior the tests don't yet demand. Document.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to next test after completion.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the current implementation and test files to understand the code I'm refactoring.
Let me first verify all tests are green before refactoring.

input.description: Run tests to verify green state

content.text:
All 9 tests green. Now I'll work through the Refactor phase systematically.

## Step 1: Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)

- **`quote`**: Clearly reveals intent — produces an insurance premium quote. Keep.
- **`runeCount`**: Clear. Keep.
- **`nonRuneBasePremium`**: Clear. Keep.
- **`runeBasePremium`**: Clear. Keep.
- **`BLOCK_SIZE` / `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE`**: Descriptive. Keep.

Naming is solid. No rename needed.

## Step 2: Calculate Initial APP Mass

```
const runeCount = items.filter((item) => item.type === "rune").length;
const nonRuneBasePremium = items
  .filter((item) => item.type !== "rune")
  .reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);
const runeBasePremium =
  runeCount === BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : runeCount * BASE_PREMIUM.rune;
return nonRuneBasePremium + runeBasePremium + POLICY_FEE;
```

Component count (function body only):
- Constants: `"rune"`, `"rune"`(in filter ≠), `0` (reduce seed) = 3
- Bindings: `items`, `item`(×3 lambda params), `runeCount`, `sum`, `nonRuneBasePremium`, `runeBasePremium` ≈ 9
- Invocations: `filter`, `length`, `filter`, `reduce`, `+`(reduce), `BASE_PREMIUM[]` access, `===`, `*`, `+`, `+` ≈ 10 → mass 20
- Conditionals: `?:` = 1 → mass 4
- Loops: `filter`, `filter`, `reduce` = 3 → mass 15
- Assignments: 0

**Total Mass ≈ 3 + 9 + 20 + 4 + 15 = 51**

## Step 3: Apply Simple Design Rules

### Rule 1: Tests Pass ✅ — all 9 green.

### Rule 2: Reveals Intent
The current code separates runes from non-runes with two passes over `items` and a special-case ternary for the block. The ternary `runeCount === BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : runeCount * 25` is a hardcoded shortcut. It reveals "3 runes cost 60", but the *why* (a block of 3 alike items is discounted) is only partially expressed.

### Rule 3: No Duplication
There is **conceptual duplication / coupling smell**: items are filtered twice (`=== "rune"` and `!== "rune"`) which is two iterations expressing one partition.

### Rule 4: Fewest Elements
Two `filter` passes plus a separate `reduce` could be reduced.

## Step 4: Implement Refactoring

The prompt asks me to evaluate whether the structure should generalize. **It must not** — the tests only demand: single rune = 25, 2 runes = 50, 3 runes = 60 (block). Tests for 4 runes, 7 runes, and moonstones are still `todo`. Generalizing the block logic now (e.g. `Math.floor(count/3)*60 + remainder*25` across all types) would be implementing ahead of the tests.

So I'll keep behavior identical and target the **duplication** (double filter) and **clarity** of the rune special-case. A single grouping pass eliminates the conceptual duplication of partitioning `items`. Let me refactor to compute the rune count and non-rune sum in one pass, and keep the block special-case clearly named.

content.text: That reordering groups the rune logic together but is cosmetic and doesn't reduce mass or duplication meaningfully. Let me reconsider — the genuine improvement available without adding behavior is eliminating the **double-filter duplication** by partitioning items in a single pass. Let me apply that.

content.text: This extracts the rune predicate so the "what makes an item a rune" knowledge has a single representation (Rule 3) — both filters now reference `isRune` instead of independently hardcoding `"rune"`. Let me verify tests stay green.

content.content:
✓ src/claim-office.spec.ts  (38 tests | 29 skipped) 2ms

   Duration  189ms (transform 27ms, setup 0ms, collect 25ms, tests 2ms, environment 0ms, prepare 55ms)

content.text:
All 9 tests still green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

```
const isRune = (item) => item.type === "rune";  // extracted predicate
```
- Constants: `"rune"` = 1
- Bindings: `isRune`, `item` = 2
- Invocations: `===`, `.type` access = ~2 → mass 4
- **Subtotal: ~7**

```
function quote(...) {
  const runeCount = items.filter(isRune).length;
  const runeBasePremium = runeCount === BLOCK_SIZE ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : runeCount * BASE_PREMIUM.rune;
  const nonRuneBasePremium = items.filter((item) => !isRune(item)).reduce((sum, item) => sum + BASE_PREMIUM[item.type], 0);
  return nonRuneBasePremium + runeBasePremium + POLICY_FEE;
}
```
- Constants: `0` (reduce seed) = 1
- Bindings: `items`, `item`(×2), `runeCount`, `sum`, `runeBasePremium`, `nonRuneBasePremium` ≈ 7
- Invocations: `filter`, `length`, `===`, `*`, `filter`, `isRune`, `!`, `reduce`, `+`, `BASE_PREMIUM[]`, `+`, `+` ≈ 12 → mass 24
- Conditionals: `?:` = 1 → mass 4
- Loops: `filter`, `filter`, `reduce` = 3 → mass 15
- **Subtotal: ~51**

**Total Mass ≈ 58** (predicate extraction added a small invocation, slightly up from 51).

The APP mass is marginally *higher* because extracting `isRune` introduces an invocation. **This is a case where Rule 3 (no duplication) and Rule 2 (reveals intent) justifiably trump APP.** The literal `"rune"` was previously written in two places, encoding the same domain knowledge twice; consolidating it into one named predicate is the correct trade-off per the priority ordering. The `+2` mass is the cost of eliminating conceptual duplication.

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied:**
- ✅ Rule 3 (No Duplication): Extracted `isRune` predicate. The knowledge "an item is a rune when `type === 'rune'`" now has a single representation, referenced by both the rune-count filter and the non-rune filter (previously `"rune"` was hardcoded in two independent comparisons).
- ✅ Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): `items.filter(isRune)` and `filter((item) => !isRune(item))` read as domain language rather than raw string comparisons.

**Deliberately NOT done (would add behavior tests don't demand):**
- Did **not** generalize the block discount across item types or to counts > 3. The `todo` tests for 4 runes (105), 7 runes (180), and moonstone blocks define the eventual general rule. Implementing a `floor(count/blockSize)` formula now would be implementing ahead of the tests. The special-case ternary stays until the next Red test forces generalization.
- Did **not** merge the two filter passes into a single partition reduce. That would lower mass slightly but obscures the two distinct concepts (count-of-runes vs sum-of-non-runes) before the block rule's true shape is known from upcoming tests.

**Mass Change:** ~51 → ~58 (Δ +7). APP rose slightly; accepted because Rule 2 and Rule 3 outrank Rule 4/APP in the priority ordering, and the change removes duplicated domain knowledge.

## Step 7: Report Completion

🔄 **Refactor Phase Complete:**
- **Refactoring:** Extracted `isRune` predicate to remove duplicated `"rune"` literal across both filters (Rule 3) and improve readability (Rule 2).
- **Mass Change:** ~51 → ~58 (+7; clarity/de-duplication intentionally chosen over raw APP).
- **Tests:** All 9 passing ✅ (29 todo).

Files:
- Implementation: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
- Tests: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-29_08-54-56_claim-office-example-mapping_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts`

The rune special-casing was intentionally left intact: generalizing it now would implement behavior the next tests (4 runes, 7 runes, moonstone blocks) are meant to drive out. Proceeding to the next test.
